---
title: "1.9 Method Signatures"
subtitle: "Using Objects and Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Methods organize complex programs

Until now, most code has been inside `main`. Complex programs are made up of many <span class="term">methods</span> (方法).

A method is a named block of code that performs a task when it is called.

::: {.tight-list}
- A <span class="term">block</span> is code enclosed in curly braces `{ }`.
- A method can solve one smaller subproblem.
- Method names should describe what the method does.
:::

## Procedural Abstraction

### Use a method without knowing every detail

<span class="term">Procedural abstraction</span> (过程抽象) lets a programmer use a method by knowing what it does, even without knowing exactly how it is implemented.

Everyday analogy:

::: {.tight-list}
- You can use a car's brakes without knowing every internal mechanical detail.
- You can call `println()` without knowing the full `PrintStream` implementation.
:::

This is how large programs stay readable.

## Repeated Code

### Repetition signals a method opportunity

The textbook uses song lyrics because repeated lines make method extraction visible.

Repeated lines in an Old MacDonald verse include:

```java
System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm");
System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");
```

When you see identical code repeated, ask whether it should become a named method.

## Method Calls

### Call the method name to run its code

A <span class="term">method call</span> includes the method name and parentheses.

```java
methodName();
System.out.println("Hello World");
```

When a method is called:

::: {.tight-list}
- flow of control jumps to the method
- the method body executes
- control returns to the line immediately after the call
:::

## Flow of Control

### Old MacDonald with methods

```java
public class OldMacDonaldSong
{
    public static void intro()
    {
        System.out.println("Old MacDonald had a farm");
        chorus();
    }

    public static void chorus()
    {
        System.out.println("E-I-E-I-O");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        intro();
        System.out.println("And on that farm they had a cow.");
        chorus();
        intro();
    }
}
```

Execution starts in `main`, then jumps through calls.

## Code Task

### Add method calls for another verse

Scroll to `main` and add a second verse by calling existing methods.

```java
public static void main(String[] args)
{
    intro();
    System.out.println("And on that farm they had a cow.");
    chorus();
    System.out.println("With a moo moo here and a moo moo there");
    System.out.println("Here a moo, there a moo, everywhere a moo moo");

    // 1. Call intro().
    // 2. Print a line with a duck or another animal.
    // 3. Call chorus().
    // 4. Print the animal sound lines.
    // 5. Call intro() again.
}
```

Completion checks: at least 3 calls to `intro()` and 3 calls to `chorus()`.

## Trace Task

### Method call output order

What does this code print?

```java
public static void print()
{
    System.out.print("I like to ");
    eat();
    eat();
    eat();
    fruit();
}
```

where `eat()` prints `eat ` and `fruit()` prints `apples and bananas!`.

Answer:

```text
I like to eat eat eat apples and bananas!
```

## Method Header

### What the caller and compiler need

The <span class="term">method header</span> is the first line of a method.

It includes:

::: {.tight-list}
- method name
- return type
- parameter list with data types
:::

For this lesson, many examples use the return type `void`, which means the method does not return a value.

## Method Signature

### Header without the return type

![Method signature and method call](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-9-method-signatures/method-parts.png){fig-align="center" width="62%"}

A <span class="term">method signature</span> consists of:

::: {.tight-list}
- method name
- ordered list of parameter types
:::

A method without parameters has the method name and an empty parameter list.

## PrintStream Examples

### Same method name, different parameter lists

Examples from `PrintStream`:

```java
void println()
void println(String x)
void println(int x)
```

Corresponding calls:

```java
System.out.println();
System.out.println("Hello World");
System.out.println(42);
```

The argument must be compatible with the parameter type in the method signature.

## Parameters and Arguments

### Header variables versus call-time values

The <span class="term">parameter</span> is declared in the method header.

The <span class="term">argument</span> is the actual value passed into the method call.

```java
void println(String x)   // x is a parameter
println("Hello World");  // "Hello World" is an argument
```

Arguments can be literals, variables, or expressions that evaluate to the correct type.

## Finding Parameters

### What changes between verses?

Old MacDonald verses repeat the same structure, but these parts change:

::: {.tight-list}
- animal: `cow`, `duck`
- sound: `moo`, `quack`
:::

Those changing values are good candidates for parameters.

This is the design move: keep the shared structure in one method, pass the changing values as arguments.

## Method with Parameters

### One method can print many verses

```java
public static void verse(String animal, String sound)
{
    System.out.println("And on this farm, they had a " + animal);
    chorus();
    System.out.println("With a " + sound + " " + sound
            + " here and a " + sound + " " + sound + " there");
    System.out.println("Here a " + sound
            + ", there a " + sound
            + ", everywhere a " + sound + " " + sound);
}
```

The parameters `animal` and `sound` hold different values for each call.

## Main as Outline

### Method calls show the program structure

```java
public static void main(String[] args)
{
    intro();
    verse("cow", "moo");
    intro();
    verse("duck", "quack");
    intro();
}
```

Main methods often read like an outline: high-level calls appear in the order the program should run.

## Code Task

### Add verses with arguments

Add a verse with animal `goose` and sound `honk`, then call `intro()` again. Repeat with another animal and sound of your choice.

```java
public static void main(String[] args)
{
    intro();
    verse("cow", "moo");
    intro();
    verse("duck", "quack");
    intro();

    // Add verse("goose", "honk");
    // Then call intro().
    // Add one more animal and sound.
}
```

Completion checks: include `verse("goose", "honk");` and at least 5 calls to `intro()`.

## Arguments to Parameters

### Matching by number, type, and order

![Arguments to parameters](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-9-method-signatures/args2paramsFarm.png){fig-align="center" width="66%"}

Java chooses the right method by checking:

::: {.tight-list}
- method name
- number of arguments
- data types of the arguments
- order of parameter types
:::

Method headers contain types; method calls contain values.

## Call by Value

### Parameters receive copies

Java uses <span class="term">call by value</span> when it passes arguments to methods.

That means:

::: {.tight-list}
- the argument's value is copied into the parameter variable
- the parameter is initialized with that copy
- changing the parameter variable does not change the original primitive variable outside the method
:::

For now, read method calls as passing values into named parameter variables.

## Overloading

### Same method name, different signatures

Methods are <span class="term">overloaded</span> when multiple methods have the same name but different signatures.

Example:

```java
println()
println(String x)
println(int x)
```

The compiler determines which version to call from the number and types of arguments.

## Groupwork Challenge

### Song with Parameters

The Ants Go Marching repeats structure with different values.

Changing phrases:

| Verse | `num` argument | `action` argument |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `"one"` | `"suck a thumb"` |
| 2 | `"two"` | `"tie a shoe"` |
| 3 | `"three"` | `"climb a tree"` |

These changing phrases become arguments to `chorus(num)` and `verse(num, action)`.

## Groupwork Challenge

### Ants Song starter

Write code in `main` that calls `chorus` and `verse` to print all three verses.

```java
public class AntsSong
{
    public static void chorus(String num)
    {
        System.out.println("The ants go marching " + num
                + " by " + num + ", hurrah, hurrah");
        System.out.println("The ants go marching " + num
                + " by " + num + ", hurrah, hurrah");
    }

    public static void verse(String num, String action)
    {
        System.out.println("The ants go marching " + num + " by " + num);
        System.out.println("The little one stops to " + action);
        System.out.println("And they all go marching down to the ground");
        System.out.println("To get out of the rain, BOOM! BOOM! BOOM! BOOM!\n");
    }
}
```

## Groupwork Challenge

### Required method calls

Your `main` method should call:

```java
chorus("one");
verse("one", "suck a thumb");

chorus("two");
verse("two", "tie a shoe");

chorus("three");
verse("three", "climb a tree");
```

A strong solution uses method calls instead of copying all song lines into `main`.

## AP-Style Trace Check

### Which calls print `purr meow purr`?

```java
public class Cat
{
    public static void sound1()
    {
        System.out.print("meow ");
    }

    public static void sound2()
    {
        System.out.print("purr ");
    }

    public static void hello()
    {
        sound2();
        sound1();
    }
}
```

Correct call sequence: `hello(); sound2();`

## Vocabulary Check

### Match the concept

| Definition | Concept |
|---|---|
| named block of code to perform a task | method |
| where execution starts | main method |
| method name and ordered parameter types | method signature |
| variable declared in a method header | parameter |
| value passed into a method call | argument |

Use this vocabulary precisely when reading API documentation.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- define a <span class="term">method</span> as a named task block
- explain <span class="term">procedural abstraction</span>
- trace jump-and-return <span class="term">flow of control</span> during method calls
- identify the method name, return type, and parameter list in a header
- define a <span class="term">method signature</span> as method name plus ordered parameter types
- distinguish <span class="term">parameters</span> from <span class="term">arguments</span>
- explain <span class="term">call by value</span> as copying argument values into parameters
- explain <span class="term">overloading</span> as same method name with different signatures
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
